pathophysiology_summary: A type IV (delayed) hypersensitivity reaction to plant allergen (e.g., urushiol/Rhus) developed ~12 hours after exposure, producing intensely pruritic erythema with tense vesicles and bullae in a linear/geometric distribution tracing where plant matter brushed the skin — a pattern characteristic of allergic contact dermatitis. Discriminators from the feared mimics: the bilateral linear exposure pattern with pruritus dominating over pain argues against dermatomal, painful herpes zoster; the clear plant-exposure history, intense itch and linear bullae argue against (painless) bullous impetigo; and the hyper-acute exposure-linked onset with no mucosal or generalised involvement argues against an autoimmune blistering disease or SJS/TEN. Management is allergen removal/decontamination, potent topical corticosteroid, itch control, and — given severity/blistering — a short oral corticosteroid course, with a safety-net for systemic allergy and secondary infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Linear vesiculobullous itch after plant exposure.
Any acute blistering eruption should be screened for mucosal/systemic involvement.
Vesicular but dermatomal and painful; here bilateral, linear and itchy.
Bullous but typically painless without an exposure pattern.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise plant ACD from the linear pattern and screen for mucosal/systemic (SJS) involvement; don't over-diagnose a systemic blistering disorder.

differential.evidence_basis: Exposure-linked linear vesiculobullae, no mucosal/systemic involvement.
No mucosal involvement, no systemic features, confined to exposed skin.
Working diagnosis: allergen removal, potent topical ± short oral steroid, itch control, infection/systemic safety-net.

red_flags.description: Mucosal involvement, facial/airway swelling, or systemic features (SJS/TEN or systemic allergy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate a life-threatening systemic blistering disorder or anaphylaxis needing emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed SJS/TEN or airway involvement can be fatal.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection (thick purulent exudate, spreading warmth, fever)

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Escalating to ED for SJS/bullous pemphigoid when the pattern is clearly exposure-linked ACD without mucosal/systemic involvement.
Topical only for extensive blistering ACD where a short oral steroid course is appropriate.
Not advising washing of clothes/tools to remove residual allergen (ongoing re-exposure).

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prescribe potent topical ± short oral corticosteroid and antihistamine, advise allergen removal/decontamination and itch care; ED for any mucosal/airway/systemic features or secondary infection.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-escalating for a localised ACD, or missing early systemic/mucosal involvement.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Severe/extensive allergic contact dermatitis: remove the allergen, apply a potent topical corticosteroid, and consider a short tapering course of oral prednis(ol)one; antihistamines for itch.
